source: ESV Study Bible (Crossway) — Used under license
book_intro: The letter to the Hebrews was written to encourage Christians in a time of trial. It does so by focusing on the absolute supremacy and sufficiency of Jesus Christ. While God spoke in the past "many times and in many ways," he has now spoken to us "by his Son," Jesus Christ, who is the "exact imprint" of God's nature and who "upholds the universe by the word of his power" (1:1-3). Jesus accomplished complete salvation for all who trust in him (1:1-10:18). We dare not "neglect such a great salvation" (2:3; 5:12-6:20; 10:19-39). Rather, in our faith and in our everyday living, we should imitate the example of Christ and of those on the honor roll of faith (chs. 11-13). This letter, whose author is unknown, was probably written between a.d. 60 and 70.
chapter_intro: Chapter 12 gathers the momentum of ch. 11 and drives it into an urgent appeal: surrounded by this cloud of witnesses who persevered, run the race before us with endurance, fixing our eyes on Jesus — the pioneer and perfecter of faith. The chapter then explains suffering as divine discipline (a sign of sonship, not abandonment), issues the fourth major warning (do not refuse him who is speaking), and closes with the majestic contrast between Sinai and Zion: law versus grace, terror versus joy, the earthly versus the heavenly Jerusalem.

passages.title: Run the Race, Fix Your Eyes on Jesus
passages.text: The 'cloud of witnesses' in v.1 is not spectators watching from the stands but witnesses whose testimony — their lives of faith — surrounds and encourages us. The call to 'lay aside every weight and sin' before running pictures a runner stripping off unnecessary encumbrances. The ultimate model: Jesus himself, who endured the cross for the joy set before him and is now seated at God's right hand.

passages.title: Discipline as Sonship
passages.text: Prov 3:11–12 reframes suffering: God disciplines those he loves. The readers have not yet resisted to the point of shedding blood in their struggle against sin; the hardships they face are not signs of abandonment but of a father-child relationship. Earthly discipline (from fallible fathers) is temporary and limited; divine discipline is purposeful and yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness.

passages.title: Sinai vs. Zion — Two Mountains, Two Responses
passages.text: The two mountains define two economies: Sinai (terror, fire, darkness, trumpet, the voice that those who heard it begged would stop) and Zion (the joyful assembly of the firstborn, the heavenly Jerusalem, God the judge, the spirits of the righteous made perfect, Jesus the mediator of the new covenant). The contrast is not law vs. no law, but the old covenant's condemning word vs. the new covenant's better-speaking blood of Jesus. The warning: do not refuse the one speaking from heaven.

notes.text: lay aside every weight — Not only explicit sin but anything that impedes progress in the race: distractions, comforts, relationships, ambitions that crowd out persevering faith.

pioneer and perfecter of our faith — archēgon (pioneer/founder): he blazed the trail of faith ahead of us. teleiōtēn (perfecter): he brought it to its goal in his own life of obedience and resurrection.
the Lord disciplines the one he loves — Pain in the Christian life is not random and is not punishment for specific sins (as Job's friends wrongly insisted). It is covenantal fathering.
the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than Abel's — Abel's blood cried for vengeance (Gen 4:10); Christ's blood speaks for forgiveness. The contrast is between two kinds of blood-cry.
a consuming fire — The same God who is our Father is also the holy Judge. Grace does not eliminate the fear of God but grounds it rightly: it is the living God before whom we stand.
